interactions.summary: Jiang answers or builds on an audience point about Caesar's murder: despite months of planning and sixty conspirators, only five could physically attack because the taboo against Senate violence was stronger than fear of death.
Jiang closes with Augustus's succession problem: Augustus wants an adoption system that selects the best relative, with Germanicus as the ideal future emperor and Tiberius as a temporary bridge.
Jiang argues that Tiberius breaks Augustus's system by killing Germanicus and his family, adopting Caligula, and thereby marking the Roman Empire as basically dead despite its later duration.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 appears to be Jiang. Segment 0041 begins after a poorly captured audience contribution in prior context, so the exact question is uncertain.

claims.claim: Only five of about sixty conspirators physically attacked Caesar because even trained soldiers and generals were paralyzed by the taboo against violence in the Senate.
claims.temporal_scope: Roman history as narrated in Jiang lecture published 2024-11-19

claims.claim: Jiang treats the first stab as a small, trembling backstab that Caesar could not initially understand as real attack because the taboo was so strong.
The assassination is world-historical because it breaks the unimaginable taboo of physical violence in the Senate against Caesar, a figure considered holy and divine by the Roman people.
Augustus presents himself not as emperor but as first citizen or first man in the Senate, responsible for the Republic's eternal prosperity and stability.
Augustus's succession solution is adoption of the most competent relative so that the best man in Rome always inherits imperial power.
Germanicus is Jiang's example of the ideal successor: Caesar-like, brilliant, and beloved by soldiers, but too young, requiring Tiberius as a transitional emperor.
Tiberius destroys Augustus's succession design by killing Germanicus and his family, then adopting Caligula, causing the system to break down quickly.
Jiang argues that Tiberius can be said to mark the death of the Roman Empire: it continues for centuries, but only through size and inertia amid internal revolt and tension.

signature_moments.moment: Sixty conspirators bring daggers, but only five can actually strike Caesar.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The repetition makes taboo visible as a force stronger than months of planning and hatred.

signature_moments.moment: The first attack is a trembling pinprick in Caesar's back, not a heroic blow.
signature_moments.source_phrase: he was shaking... pinprick Caesar in the back
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The image strips the assassination of grandeur and makes it an act of taboo violation before it is an act of courage.

signature_moments.moment: The fear was not death; it was bringing the ultimate taboo into the Senate.
signature_moments.source_phrase: There wasn't fear of death. There was this fear of bringing this ultimate taboo
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This differentiates sacred fear from physical cowardice and closes the earlier taboo argument.

signature_moments.moment: Augustus's dream is an adoption machine for greatness: the best man in Rome would always be adopted into the throne.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the best man in Rome would always be adopted into the imperial throne
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The succession system is the institutional version of Caesar's talent principle, meant to preserve empire without heredity's decay.

signature_moments.moment: The Empire keeps moving for three hundred years after it is basically dead, carried by size and inertia.
signature_moments.source_phrase: it was basically dead... only because of its size and inertia
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang's historical afterimage: institutions can survive long after the principle that animated them has collapsed.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang links the idea to the first conspirator's trembling attack from behind Caesar.
Augustus's self-presentation as first citizen or first man in the Senate rather than openly as emperor.
Augustus's intended system of adopting the most competent relative so imperial power passes to the best man.
Jiang's explanation for how the Roman Empire could continue for centuries after, in his argument, it was basically dead under Tiberius.

chronology_notes.note: The lecture closes by returning from Caesar's assassination taboo to Augustus's failed succession design and the early death of Rome's imperial principle.

uncertainty_notes.note: The audience question or comment before Jiang's answer about the difficulty of killing Caesar is not directly captured; only Jiang's response is usable.
Segment 0043 contains significant ASR damage around the phrase about 'ultimate taboo' and the Senate; extracted claims rely on the surrounding coherent wording.
